text: In the field of high-dimensional credit card fraud detection, the quality of the model performance, and therefore its interpretability, largely depends on the feature selection. This research compares two popular feature selection methods — intrinsic feature importance and SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations) values — using five classifiers — XGBoost, Decision Tree, CatBoost, Extremely Randomized Trees, and Random Forest. The performances of the models are evaluated based on the Area under the Precision-Recall Curve (AUPRC) metric. The experiments are carried out on the Kaggle Credit Card Fraud Detection dataset and the results are validated using statistical tests. The results show that the models that are trained using the intrinsic feature importance outperform the models that are trained using SHAP values for all subset sizes of features. Hence, the intrinsic feature importance is recommended for large data sets. The intrinsic feature importance is a faster alternative, as it is calculated during the training of the model, and SHAP value calculation is computationally more expensive.
